subtopic_detail: Cannabis-induced psychosis vs first episode schizophrenia
stem: A 19-year-old male is admitted with 5 days of persecutory delusions about being followed by police and second-person command auditory hallucinations. He uses high-potency cannabis daily and his last use was 12 hours ago. He has had previous brief paranoid episodes after heavy cannabis sessions that resolved within 48 hours of cessation. His older brother was diagnosed with schizophrenia at age 22.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: marked paranoia, third-person auditory hallucinations, blunted affect, formal thought disorder; appears internally preoccupied
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen positive for cannabis only; CT brain normal; TSH and electrolytes normal; collateral confirms no premorbid functional decline before recent cannabis escalation 9 months ago

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate diagnostic and management approach now?

options.text: Diagnose first episode schizophrenia and commence long-acting paliperidone palmitate intramuscular depot before discharge home with community mental health follow-up
options.rationale: Premature labelling and depot initiation before observing the post-substance withdrawal trajectory is not best practice; substance-induced psychosis often resolves with abstinence. (Source: RANZCP: First Episode Psychosis Guideline)

options.text: Diagnose cannabis intoxication and discharge home today with cessation counselling alone and review at the local community mental health team within 1 month
options.rationale: Acute psychosis with command hallucinations requires admission for safety and observation regardless of substance use cause. (Source: RANZCP: First Episode Psychosis Guideline)

options.text: Admit to a specialist youth early psychosis service for low-dose oral antipsychotic, cannabis cessation support, and longitudinal review for emerging psychosis
options.rationale: Australian early intervention practice supports admission, low-dose oral antipsychotic, structured cannabis cessation, and longitudinal review because up to 40% of cannabis-induced psychoses convert to schizophrenia. (Source: Orygen: First Episode Psychosis)

options.text: Diagnose substance-induced psychotic disorder definitively and avoid antipsychotic medication entirely while encouraging abstinence over the next 4 weeks
options.rationale: Antipsychotic medication relieves distressing symptoms and reduces risk during active psychosis even when substance use is implicated. (Source: RANZCP: First Episode Psychosis Guideline)

options.text: Commence high-dose intramuscular zuclopenthixol acuphase 100 mg with physical restraint maintained for the duration of all acute behavioural symptoms
options.rationale: Acuphase is reserved for severe acute behavioural disturbance unresponsive to oral therapy; oral first-line antipsychotic is preferred when the patient can engage. (Source: NSW Health: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

explanation.summary: Cannabis-induced psychosis and first episode schizophrenia can be indistinguishable cross-sectionally. Australian early intervention practice favours admission to a specialist youth early psychosis service, low-dose oral antipsychotic to manage acute symptoms, structured cannabis cessation support, and longitudinal review. Approximately 40% of cannabis-induced psychoses convert to schizophrenia within 5 years, particularly with high-potency cannabis exposure, family history, and prodromal functional decline. Early intervention reduces transition rates and improves outcomes.
key_points: Admit acute psychosis with command hallucinations regardless of substance use
Use low-dose oral antipsychotic plus structured cannabis cessation
Up to 40% of cannabis-induced psychosis converts to schizophrenia within 5 years
explanation.pearls: Family history of schizophrenia and prodromal functional decline strongly raise transition risk after cannabis-induced psychosis; track these explicitly.

subtopic_detail: Augmentation strategy in treatment-resistant unipolar depression
stem: A 52-year-old has had inadequate response to two adequate trials of antidepressant: sertraline 200 mg daily for 10 weeks then venlafaxine extended release 225 mg daily for 12 weeks. She has a current Hamilton Depression Rating Scale of 22. She remains in partial remission with persistent low mood, anhedonia, and middle insomnia. She has no psychotic features, no bipolarity in personal or family history, and no suicidal ideation. Her thyroid function, vitamin B12, and full blood count are normal.

data_table.Examination: BMI 27, BP 124/78, no parkinsonism, no acute distress
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.9 mIU/L, B12 320 pmol/L, fasting glucose 4.8 mmol/L, lithium not previously tried, cardiac echo normal, ECG QTc 410 ms

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Cross-titrate to oral fluoxetine 60 mg daily over 2 weeks before reassessing for a further antidepressant switch
options.rationale: A third sequential antidepressant monotherapy switch has lower remission rates than augmentation after two adequate antidepressant trials. (Source: RANZCP: Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP: Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Augment with aripiprazole 2.5 mg daily, titrating to 10 mg as tolerated, with metabolic and akathisia monitoring at 4 and 8 weeks
options.rationale: Atypical antipsychotic augmentation, typically aripiprazole or quetiapine, has the strongest evidence base for treatment-resistant unipolar depression in current Australian guidance. (Source: RANZCP: Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP: Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Combine venlafaxine with a tricyclic antidepressant such as amitriptyline 75 mg nightly to maximise noradrenergic effect over the next 6 weeks
options.rationale: Combining serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor with tricyclic antidepressant has significant serotonergic and cardiovascular risk and is not a routine augmentation. (Source: AMH: Antidepressants)

options.text: Augment with the dopamine agonist pramipexole 0.125 mg three times daily as a first-line augmentation strategy
options.rationale: Pramipexole augmentation has limited evidence and is not first-line in current Australian guidance. (Source: RANZCP: Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP: Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Recommend immediate electroconvulsive therapy as initial next step without further pharmacological augmentation trials
options.rationale: Electroconvulsive therapy is appropriate for severe, psychotic, or treatment-resistant depression but is generally considered after augmentation trials in non-psychotic moderate cases. (Source: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive Therapy Guideline)

explanation.summary: Treatment-resistant unipolar depression after two adequate antidepressant trials benefits more from augmentation than a third sequential monotherapy switch. Atypical antipsychotic augmentation with aripiprazole or quetiapine has the strongest evidence base and is preferred in current Australian guidance. Lithium augmentation remains an option, particularly with comorbid suicidal ideation. Electroconvulsive therapy is considered for severe, psychotic, or treatment-resistant depression unresponsive to augmentation. Monitor metabolic side effects, akathisia, and movement disorders during augmentation.
key_points: After two adequate antidepressant trials, augment rather than switch
Aripiprazole and quetiapine have the strongest augmentation evidence
Monitor for metabolic syndrome and akathisia during antipsychotic augmentation
explanation.pearls: Quetiapine extended release has additional sedative benefit in middle insomnia; aripiprazole is more activating and may worsen insomnia in some patients.

sources.label: RANZCP: Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline

stem: A 32-year-old has been stable on clozapine 350 mg nightly for 8 months for treatment-resistant schizophrenia. His routine weekly bloods today show an absolute neutrophil count of 1.4 x10^9/L, down from 2.1 x10^9/L a week ago. White cell count is 3.0. He is afebrile, asymptomatic, and has had no recent infections. He uses no other myelosuppressive medications. He continues to attend his weekly clozapine clinic.

data_table.Examination: No oral ulceration, no lymphadenopathy, no infective focus, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Today: WCC 3.0, neutrophils 1.4 x10^9/L, lymphocytes 1.3, platelets 220, Hb 142; previous week: WCC 4.1, neutrophils 2.1 x10^9/L; clozapine level 540 microgram/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next action?

options.text: Continue clozapine unchanged and resume routine 4-weekly bloods after a single repeat full blood count tomorrow morning
options.rationale: An absolute neutrophil count below 1.5 x10^9/L requires increased haematological surveillance regardless of asymptomatic status. (Source: ClozapinePMC Australia Protocol)

options.text: Stop clozapine permanently and switch to oral aripiprazole 10 mg daily, starting cross-titration today before discharge home
options.rationale: Permanent cessation is reserved for the red zone with absolute neutrophil count below 1.0 x10^9/L or other evidence of agranulocytosis. (Source: ClozapinePMC Australia Protocol)

options.text: Hospitalise and commence intravenous filgrastim 300 microgram daily until neutrophil recovery without changing the clozapine dose
options.rationale: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor is reserved for the red zone with severe neutropenia, not amber zone monitoring; clozapine should be ceased in the red zone. (Source: ClozapinePMC Australia Protocol)

options.text: Continue clozapine at the current dose, repeat full blood count twice weekly, and notify the clozapine programme as the patient is now in the amber zone
options.rationale: Absolute neutrophil count between 1.0 and 1.5 x10^9/L is the amber zone; continue clozapine, increase monitoring to twice weekly, and notify the clozapine programme. (Source: ClozapinePMC Australia Protocol)

options.text: Halve the clozapine dose to 175 mg nightly and continue routine 4-weekly bloods, with general practitioner review only if febrile illness emerges
options.rationale: Dose reduction does not reverse clozapine-induced neutropenia; increased monitoring frequency is required and routine 4-weekly bloods are inadequate. (Source: ClozapinePMC Australia Protocol)

explanation.summary: Clozapine haematological monitoring uses a traffic light system. Absolute neutrophil count over 2.0 x10^9/L is green and continues routine monitoring. Counts between 1.5 and 2.0 x10^9/L are yellow with twice-weekly bloods and physician review. Counts between 1.0 and 1.5 x10^9/L are amber with twice-weekly bloods, continued clozapine, and clozapine programme notification. Counts below 1.0 x10^9/L are red and clozapine is ceased; granulocyte colony-stimulating factor and intravenous antibiotics are considered if febrile.
key_points: Green over 2.0, yellow 1.5 to 2.0, amber 1.0 to 1.5, red below 1.0 x10^9/L for absolute neutrophil count
Cease clozapine in red zone and consider granulocyte colony-stimulating factor
Notify the clozapine programme for amber or red zone events
explanation.pearls: Benign ethnic neutropenia in some Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander, African, and Middle Eastern patients warrants programme-approved lower thresholds rather than clozapine cessation.

subtopic_detail: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax with moderate rim
stem: A 23-year-old tall thin male non-smoker presents with 6 hours of right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild breathlessness at rest. He has no preceding trauma. He has had no prior episodes, no underlying lung disease, and no recent travel. He is haemodynamically stable. He has no asthma history and lives 30 minutes from the hospital.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 98, RR 22, T 36.7, BP 124/76, SpO2 94% room air
data_table.Examination: Reduced breath sounds and hyperresonance on the right; trachea midline; no surgical emphysema
data_table.Investigations: Erect chest x-ray: right pneumothorax with rim of 3 cm at the hilum, no mediastinal shift; computed tomography not performed; no contralateral lung disease

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Insert an 8 to 14 French intercostal catheter into the safe triangle with underwater seal drainage and admit for observation
options.rationale: A primary spontaneous pneumothorax with rim of 2 cm or more or symptomatic at rest warrants intercostal catheter drainage in Australian practice. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pneumothorax)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pneumothorax

options.text: Aspirate with a 16-gauge cannula at the second intercostal space midclavicular line and discharge if successful
options.rationale: Needle aspiration is an option for small primary pneumothoraces but is less effective than intercostal catheter for rims of 2 cm or more with significant symptoms. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pneumothorax)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pneumothorax

options.text: Provide oxygen by face mask and observe for 24 hours without any active intervention before considering drainage
options.rationale: Conservative observation alone is for small asymptomatic primary pneumothoraces with rim less than 2 cm and reliable follow-up. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pneumothorax)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pneumothorax

options.text: Refer for urgent video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery and pleurodesis on this first presentation today
options.rationale: Definitive surgery is reserved for second ipsilateral pneumothorax, persistent air leak, contralateral history, or occupational indication. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pneumothorax)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pneumothorax

options.text: Perform emergent needle decompression at the second intercostal space midclavicular line immediately without further imaging
options.rationale: Needle decompression is reserved for tension pneumothorax with haemodynamic compromise or tracheal deviation, neither of which is present. (Source: ANZCOR: Adult Advanced Life Support)

explanation.summary: Australian guidance recommends sizing pneumothorax by interpleural distance at the hilum or apex. Rim less than 2 cm and asymptomatic suits conservative observation. Rim of 2 cm or more or symptomatic warrants intercostal catheter, often a small bore 8 to 14 French catheter. Needle aspiration is an alternative but with lower success in larger rims. Tension pneumothorax with tracheal deviation, haemodynamic compromise, or hypoxia requires immediate needle decompression and chest drain placement.
key_points: Rim less than 2 cm and asymptomatic: observe with oxygen
Rim of 2 cm or more or symptomatic: insert intercostal catheter
Tension pneumothorax: immediate needle decompression then drain
explanation.pearls: Smoking cessation is the single most important secondary prevention measure after primary spontaneous pneumothorax; the risk of recurrence is halved.

sources.label: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand: Pneumothorax

subtopic_detail: Lower limb cellulitis empirical antibiotic choice
stem: A 58-year-old presents with 24 hours of right calf erythema, warmth, and tenderness extending from the ankle to the knee with associated low-grade fever. He has type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and no known drug allergies. There is no obvious break in the skin and no fluctuance or crepitus. Both legs are otherwise clinically equal in size with no calf tenderness on Homan's manoeuvre.

data_table.Examination: Right calf 2 cm of advancing erythema with warmth and tenderness; no skin necrosis, no crepitus, no lymphangitic streaking; pulses normal; sensation intact
data_table.Investigations: FBC WCC 12.5 with neutrophilia; CRP 84; eGFR 78; HbA1c 7.6; venous duplex Doppler not yet performed but clinically deep vein thrombosis low likelihood

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial antibiotic plan?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 7 days with same-day general practitioner review of mark and limb elevation
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is broader spectrum and not first-line for uncomplicated cellulitis without diabetic foot ulcer or animal bite. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: cellulitis)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg twice daily for 10 days as first-line treatment for community-acquired soft tissue infection
options.rationale: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is reserved for suspected community-acquired methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus with purulent infection, not non-purulent cellulitis. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: cellulitis)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading dose then 15 mg/kg twice daily as initial empirical therapy until culture results return
options.rationale: Empirical vancomycin is reserved for severe infection, suspected methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, or beta-lactam allergy. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: cellulitis)

options.text: Oral clindamycin 450 mg three times daily for 7 days as first-line for non-purulent cellulitis in this presentation
options.rationale: Clindamycin is an alternative in penicillin allergy; it is not first-line in patients tolerant of beta-lactams. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: cellulitis)

options.text: Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5 days, with marked limb edge for surveillance and review at 48 hours
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin oral 500 mg four times daily covers Streptococcus and methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus for uncomplicated non-purulent cellulitis; recent Australian guidance supports 5 days for clinically improving cases. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: cellulitis)

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated non-purulent cellulitis is most commonly caused by Streptococcus species and methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. Australian first-line therapy is oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5 days in patients without severity criteria, allergy, or risk factors for community-acquired methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. Marking the erythema edge and reviewing at 48 hours guides response. Intravenous therapy is reserved for severe infection, sepsis criteria, or failure of oral therapy.
key_points: Flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5 days is first-line for uncomplicated cellulitis
Mark the erythema edge and review at 48 hours
Vancomycin and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole are reserved for specific risk factors
explanation.pearls: Recurrent leg cellulitis often reflects tinea pedis as the portal of entry; treat the fungal foot infection to prevent further episodes.
